items.q: What are Aboriginal dot paintings usually about?
items.correct: Dreamtime stories and knowledge of the land, often shown as if seen from above.
items.misconception: Random decorative patterns with no meaning.
items.why: Students see abstract dots as mere decoration, missing that the symbols encode stories and country.

items.q: Were African masks made mainly to hang on walls as art?
items.correct: No — most were made to be worn in ceremonies, dances and rituals.
items.misconception: Yes — they were carved as gallery decorations.
items.random_wrong: No — they were used as cooking tools.
items.why: Students meet masks displayed in museums and assume display was their purpose, missing their ceremonial function.

items.q: Is it fine to copy sacred symbols from another culture into your own art?
items.correct: Care is needed — some symbols are sacred or restricted, so research their meaning and treat them with respect first.
items.misconception: Yes — any image is free to use however you like.
items.random_wrong: No — you may never look at other cultures' art.
items.why: Students treat cultural imagery as free clip-art, unaware that some designs carry sacred or restricted meanings.

items.q: Dot painting belongs to which culture?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Aboriginal. “Inuit” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What are African masks often used in?
items.why: It's Ceremony. “Cooking” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Roughly how many thousand years old is Aboriginal art tradition (approximate)?
items.why: The real figure is 40. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
